g.data-narration: Welcome to Parse Trees! Today we'll explore how compilers analyze the structure of code using tree representations.
g.text: Concrete Syntax Trees in Compiler Design

g.data-narration: A parse tree is an ordered, rooted tree representing the syntactic structure of code according to grammar rules.
g.text: • Bridge between lexical and semantic analysis

g.data-narration: Every parse tree has three key components: the root (start symbol), internal nodes (non-terminals), and leaves (terminals).
Parse trees have five key properties: root is always start symbol, leaves are terminals, internal nodes are non-terminals, children follow production rules, and yield equals input.
g.text: 1. Root: Always the start symbol of grammar
2. Leaves: All terminal symbols (tokens) from input
4. Children: Correspond to RHS of a production rule
5. Yield: Reading leaves left-to-right gives input string
• Each internal node A with children representing
string α implies production A → α exists

g.data-narration: Let's examine a classic arithmetic expression grammar used for parsing expressions like id + id * id.
g.text: E = Expression, T = Term, F = Factor

g.data-narration: Leftmost derivation replaces the leftmost non-terminal at each step. For id + id * id, we expand systematically.
g.text: Leftmost Derivation for: id + id * id

g.data-narration: Here's the complete parse tree showing how the input string derives from the start symbol E through multiple levels.
g.text: Parse Tree for: id + id * id

g.data-narration: A grammar is ambiguous if it produces more than one parse tree for the same input string, causing semantic conflicts.
g.text: Definition: A grammar is ambiguous if ∃ w ∈ L(G) such that w has two or more distinct parse trees.
Problem: Ambiguous grammars cannot be used for deterministic parsing - same input has different meanings!
Example: if a then if b then c else d

g.data-narration: The if-then-else statement shows ambiguity - the else can bind to either the inner or outer if, creating different parse trees.
g.text: Input: if a then if b then c else d
Tree 1: if a then (if b then c else d)
Tree 2: (if a then if b then c) else d
Solution: Use disambiguation rules (else matches nearest then) or transform grammar

g.data-narration: While parse trees include all grammar details, Abstract Syntax Trees (AST) simplify by removing unnecessary nodes.
g.text: AST is a simplified representation that removes unnecessary nodes:

g.data-narration: Compare the parse tree (left) with all nodes versus the AST (right) with only essential operators - the AST is much cleaner.
Leftmost and rightmost derivations produce the same parse tree structure - they only differ in the order of expansions.
g.text: Leftmost Derivation: Replace leftmost non-terminal at each step
Rightmost Derivation: Replace rightmost non-terminal at each step
Key Insight: Both produce the SAME parse tree structure - only the order of expansion differs!

g.data-narration: Remember these key relationships: every parse tree corresponds to one leftmost and one rightmost derivation.
g.text: Parse Tree → Derivation: Every parse tree corresponds to exactly one leftmost derivation
Derivation → Parse Tree: A leftmost derivation uniquely determines a parse tree structure

g.data-narration: In summary: parse trees represent syntactic structure with root (start symbol), internal nodes (non-terminals), and leaves (terminals).
g.text: Root = Start Symbol | Internal Nodes = Non-terminals | Leaves = Terminals | Yield = Input
Parse trees bridge lexical and semantic analysis | ASTs are simplified versions | Ambiguous grammars need resolution
